title: Identify quadratic patterns
description: Given a polynomial expression whose degree is more than 2, determine whether it can be factored using one of the common quadratic patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$(x+7)^2+2y(x+7)+y^2$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $(x+7)^2+2y(x+7)+y^2$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

Since all the terms in $(x+7)^2+2y(x+7)+y^2$ are positive, the relevant pattern to consider is $U^2+2UV+V^2$.

If we set $U=(x+7)$ then we get:

$(x+7)^2+2(x+7)V+V^2$

Now we can set $V=y$ and we get our expression:

$(x+7)^2+2y(x+7)+y^2$
So we know we can use the pattern $(U+V)^2$.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$(x-3)^2-10(x-3)+25$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $(x-3)^2-10(x-3)+25$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

Since the middle term in $(x-3)^2-10(x-3)+25$ is negative, the relevant pattern to consider is $U^2-2UV+V^2$.

If we set $U=(x-3)$ then we get:

$(x-3)^2-2(x-3)V+V^2$

Now we can set $V=5$ and we get our expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(x-3)^2-2(x-3)(5)+5^2
\\\\
&=(x-3)^2-10(x-3)+25
\end{align}$
So we know we can use the pattern $(U-V)^2$.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$x^4-14x^2+49$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $x^4-14x^2+49$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

Since the middle term in $x^4-14x^2+49$ is negative, the relevant pattern to consider is $U^2-2UV+V^2$.

If we set $U=x^2$ then we get:

$(x^2)^2-2(x^2)V+V^2$

Now we can set $V=7$ and we get our expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(x^2)^2-2(x^2)(7)+7^2
\\\\
&=x^4-14x^2+49
\end{align}$
So we know we can use the pattern $(U-V)^2$.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$9x^6+6x^3y+y^2$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $9x^6+6x^3y+y^2$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

Since all the terms in $9x^6+6x^3y+y^2$ are positive, the relevant pattern to consider is $U^2+2UV+V^2$.

If we set $U=3x^3$ then we get:

$(3x^3)^2+2(3x^3)V+V^2$

Now we can set $V=y$ and we get our expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(3x^3)^2+2(3x^3)(y)+y^2
\\\\
&=9x^6+6x^3y+y^2
\end{align}$
So we know we can use the pattern $(U+V)^2$.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$x^4+10x+25$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $x^4+10x+25$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

Since all the terms in $x^4+10x+25$ are positive, the relevant pattern to consider is $U^2+2UV+V^2$.

If we set $U=x^2$ then we get:

$(x^2)^2+2(x^2)V+V^2$

If we set $V=5$ then we get:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(x^2)^2+2(x^2)(5)+5^2
\\\\
&=x^4+10x^2+25
\end{align}$

Notice that we need to set $U=x^2$ to get a first term of $x^4$, but that produces a middle term of $10x^2$ instead of $10x$. So this pattern doesn't match our expression.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a binomial, while $x^4+10x+25$ is a trinomial. So we can rule this pattern out.
We can't use any of the patterns.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$(x+5)^4+2(x+5)^2-1$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $(x+5)^4+2(x+5)^2-1$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

Both of these patterns will have a positive final term since $V^2$ must be positive, but $(x+5)^4+2(x+5)^2-1$ has a negative final term. So we can rule these patterns out.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a binomial, while $(x+5)^4+2(x+5)^2-1$ is a trinomial. So we can rule this pattern out.
We can't use any of the patterns.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$x^4+3x^2y+9y^2$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $x^4+3x^2y+9y^2$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

Since all the terms in $x^4+3x^2y+9y^2$ are positive, the relevant pattern to consider is $U^2+2UV+V^2$.

If we set $U=x^2$ then we get:

$(x^2)^2+2(x^2)V+V^2$

If we set $V=3y$ then we get:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(x^2)^2+2(x^2)(3y)+(3y)^2
\\\\
&=x^4+6x^2y+9y^2
\end{align}$

Notice that we need to set $U=x^2$ to get a first term of $x^4$ and $V=3y$ to get a last term of $9y^2$, but that produces a middle term of $6x^2y$ instead of $3x^2y$. So this pattern doesn't match our expression.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a binomial, while $x^4+3x^2y+9y^2$ is a trinomial. So we can rule this pattern out.
We can't use any of the patterns.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$(x-1)^2+(x-1)+4$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $(x-1)^2+(x-1)+4$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

Since all the terms in $(x-1)^2+(x-1)+4$ are positive, the relevant pattern to consider is $U^2+2UV+V^2$.

If we set $U=x-1$ then we get:

$(x-1)^2+2(x-1)V+V^2$

If we set $V=2$ then we get:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(x-1)^2+2(x-1)(2)+2^2
\\\\
&=(x-1)^2+4(x-1)+4
\end{align}$

Notice that we need to set $U=x-1$ to get a first term of $(x-1)^2$ and $V=2$ to get a last term of $4$, but that produces a middle term of $4(x-1)$ instead of $(x-1)$. So this pattern doesn't match our expression.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a binomial, while $(x-1)^2+(x-1)+4$ is a trinomial. So we can rule this pattern out.
We can't use any of the patterns.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$4x^2-9y^6$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $4x^2-9y^6$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a trinomial, while $4x^2-9y^6$ is a binomial. So we can rule these patterns out.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

If we set $U=2x$ then we get:

$(2x)^2-V^2=4x^2-V^2$

Now we can set $V=3y^3$ and we get our expression:

$4x^2-(3y^3)^2=4x^2-9y^6$
So we know we can use the pattern $(U+V)(U-V)$.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$16x^4-25$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $16x^4-25$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a trinomial, while $16x^4-25$ is a binomial. So we can rule these patterns out.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

If we set $U=4x^2$ then we get:

$(4x^2)^2-V^2=16x^4-V^2$

Now we can set $V=5$ and we get our expression:

$16x^4-(5)^2=16x^4-25$
So we know we can use the pattern $(U+V)(U-V)$.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$(x+1)^2-4y^2$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $(x+1)^2-4y^2$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a trinomial, while $(x+1)^2-4y^2$ is a binomial. So we can rule these patterns out.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

If we set $U=x+1$ then we get:

$(x+1)^2-V^2$

Now we can set $V=2y$ and we get our expression:

$(x+1)^2-(2y)^2=(x+1)^2-4y^2$
So we know we can use the pattern $(U+V)(U-V)$.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$(x-2)^4-(y+3)^4$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $(x-2)^4-(y+3)^4$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a trinomial, while $(x-2)^4-(y+3)^4$ is a binomial. So we can rule these patterns out.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

If we set $U=(x-2)^2$ then we get:

$\left((x-2)^2\right)^2-V^2=(x-2)^4-V^2$

Now we can set $V=(y+3)^2$ and we get our expression:

$(x-2)^4-\left((y+3)^2\right)^2=(x-2)^4-(y+3)^4$
So we know we can use the pattern $(U+V)(U-V)$.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$9x^4+4y^2$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $9x^4+4y^2$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a trinomial, while $9x^4+4y^2$ is a binomial. So we can rule these patterns out.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

If we set $U=3x^2$ then we get:

$(3x^2)^2-V^2=9x^4-V^2$

If we set $V=2y$ then we get:

$9x^4-(2y)^2=9x^4-4y^2$

Notice that this isn't the same as $9x^4+4y^2$, because one is a difference and one is a sum.

Actually, no matter what we set $V$ to be, we will get a difference. So this pattern doesn't match our expression either.
We can't use any of the patterns.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$x^4+9$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $x^4+9$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a trinomial, while $x^4+9$ is a binomial. So we can rule these patterns out.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

If we set $U=x^2$ then we get:

$(x^2)^2-V^2=x^4-V^2$

If we set $V=3$ then we get:

$x^4-(3)^2=x^4-9$

Notice that this isn't the same as $x^4+9$, because one is a difference and one is a sum.

Actually, no matter what we set $V$ to be, we will get a difference. So this pattern doesn't match our expression either.
We can't use any of the patterns.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$3x^2-16y^5$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $3x^2-16y^5$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a trinomial, while $3x^2-16y^5$ is a binomial. So we can rule these patterns out.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

Notice that $3$ isn't a perfect square, so there's no $U$ with integer coefficients that will get us $U^2=3x^2$. Also, $y^5$ isn't a perfect square, so there's no $V$ with integer exponents that will get us $V^2=16y^5$.So this pattern doesn't match our expression either.
We can't use any of the patterns.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.

assessment_items.question_content:
We want to factor the following expression:

$x^3-25$

**Which pattern can we use to factor the expression?**  
*$U$ and $V$ are either constant integers or single-variable expressions.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
This is how the patterns look when we expand them:

$\begin{align}
(U+V)^2&=U^2+2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U-V)^2&=U^2-2UV+V^2
\\\\
(U+V)(U-V)&=U^2-V^2
\end{align}$

Now let's consider each expanded pattern and see if it's possible to define expressions $U$ and $V$ that make the pattern equal to $x^3-25$.

## The patterns $U^2+2UV+V^2$ and $U^2-2UV+V^2$

No matter what we set $U$ and $V$ to be, we will end up with a trinomial, while $x^3-25$ is a binomial. So we can rule these patterns out.

## The pattern $U^2-V^2$

Notice that $x^3$ isn't a perfect square, so there's no $U$ with integer exponents that will get us $U^2=x^3$. So this pattern doesn't match our expression either.
We can't use any of the patterns.

choices.content: We can't use any of the patterns.
